tasks.question: I'm getting up to speed on Row Pattern Recognition support in PostgreSQL. Who is proposing and reviewing this feature, what patches exist, how does RPR differ from standard window functions, and what are the main implementation challenges around window frame reduction and the PREV/NEXT function design?
key_points: Tatsuo Ishii proposes PoC patch implementing RPR from SQL:2016, specifically Feature R020 (WINDOW clause portion), with Feature R010 (FROM clause) not yet covered
RPR enables pattern matching on time-series data (e.g., finding V-shaped stock price movements) using PATTERN and DEFINE clauses with row pattern variables
Vik Fearing points out critical design issue: RPR must reduce the window frame when calculating matched rows, unlike standard window functions which operate over full frames unconditionally
rpr() function differs from first_value() by only returning values from first row of matched pattern; unmatched rows return NULL
V2 patch adds AFTER MATCH SKIP PAST LAST ROW and AFTER MATCH SKIP TO NEXT ROW clauses controlling how pattern matching resumes after a match
Current implementation uses tweaked var->varno with OUTER_VAR and INNER_VAR to implement PREV/NEXT functions in DEFINE expressions; Ishii notes this is temporary hack needing replacement
Ishii plans to move pattern matching logic into nodeWindowAgg, specifically modifying update_frameheadpos() and update_frametailpos() to apply frame restriction to all window functions/aggregates
Pattern parsing currently happens in raw parser for simple cases, limiting support for complex quantifiers and operators; Ishii considers accepting pattern elements as Sconst instead
Jacob Champion (Timescale) identifies differences between RPR in FROM clause vs WINDOW clause regarding ONE ROW/ALL ROWS PER MATCH handling; questions if rewriting between them is always possible
Vik Fearing notes MEASURES clause required by standard, with qualified column names (e.g., LAST(DOWN.price) AS lowest) constrained to MEASURES context outside window specification

patches: RPR PoC patch (v1)
RPR v2 patch with AFTER MATCH SKIP support
concepts: AFTER MATCH SKIP PAST LAST ROW
AFTER MATCH SKIP TO NEXT ROW

tasks.question: I'm getting up to speed on pg_upgrade's handling of the pg_commit_ts directory when track_commit_timestamp is enabled. Who's involved, what's the patch doing, what code review concerns exist, and how should pg_upgrade behave when the new cluster doesn't have track_commit_timestamp enabled?
key_points: ls7777 provided a patch to automatically copy the pg_commit_ts directory during pg_upgrade and update oldestCommitTsXid and newestCommitTsXid checkpoint values when track_commit_timestamp is on
Currently pg_commit_ts is NOT copied by pg_upgrade, causing data loss; users must manually copy and run pg_resetwal (per Maxim Orlov's observation)
Hayato Kuroda identified a critical issue: track_commit_timestamp must be enabled on the NEW instance, otherwise the copied pg_commit_ts data would be removed on startup

Amit Kapila proposed following the pattern of check_new_cluster_replication_slots(): error out if track_commit_timestamp is not set on the new server but valid commit_ts data exists in the old cluster
Maxim Orlov and Amit Kapila agreed that erroring is better than silent data loss, unlike the original patch which would silently drop pg_commit_ts if conditions aren't met
Hayato Kuroda's code review noted: pgindent must be run on the patch, control data attributes must be initialized following existing patterns, indent issues in the if statement
ls7777 stated commit_ts loss is not critical and left as user responsibility in initial proposal, but later agreed to implement parameter checks and ERROR messages after community feedback
Hayato Kuroda questioned whether pg_commit_ts is user data (should be preserved) or system data (acceptable to drop), requesting community opinions
Bruce Momjian clarified that translation work is not the patch author's responsibility—language teams will handle it
Code logic concern: need to clarify if it's possible for only either oldestCommitTs or newestCommitTs to exist (not both), affecting the ternary operator logic
The patch modifies copy_subdir_files() call with conditional based on chkpnt_oldstCommitTsxid > 0, requiring proper indentation fixes

patches: Patch for migration of the pg_commit_ts directory (by ls7777, under review)

tasks.threadSubject: Patch for migration of the pg_commit_ts directory

tasks.seed: Conflict log history table for Logical Replication
tasks.question: I'm getting up to speed on the conflict log history proposal for logical replication. Who is driving this, what are the main design debates, what storage approaches are being considered, should it be a catalog or user table, and what permission/retention models are emerging?
key_points: Dilip Kumar proposed a conflict log history table to replace unstructured text logging, addressing limitations: unqueryable plain text, lack of structured queryable conflict data (table, operation, old/new data, LSN), and poor integration with external monitoring tools
Design question 1: Storage format — JSON column vs. anyarray (like pg_statistic); JSON chosen for flexibility across different table schemas

Design question 2: Catalog table vs. user table — Shveta Malik showed pg_statistic experiment: 1000 tables with 1000 rows each → 6,412 pg_statistic rows (~5.3 MB), suggesting catalogs can scale; BDR has bdr.conflict_history as extension-managed table with 30-day retention

Amit Kapila decided it must be a user table (not system catalog) for: (a) ever-growing data requiring user control to remove old data; (b) future user-controlled partitioning strategy; (c) users specifying what information to store; (d) consistency with parallel proposal for COPY error logging

Auto-creation during CREATE SUBSCRIPTION proposed: one conflict table per subscription, created automatically with options; Alastair Turner suggested CREATE ... IF NOT EXISTS semantics to allow reusing existing tables
Permission model: subscription owner receives SELECT, DELETE, TRUNCATE permissions but NOT INSERT/UPDATE to prevent manual data corruption
Alastair Turner proposed dependency relationship between conflict table and subscription to prevent accidental removal even by superusers
Retention strategy: conflict_stats_retention_period parameter proposed (default 30 days) with automatic or manual purging via purge_conflict_stats() function
Critical consideration: prevent conflict history tables from being replicated when included in FOR ALL TABLES publications
Amit Kapila suggested first version enables auto-creation with options; future versions would allow specifying existing tables; typed tables concept referenced as possible extension mechanism
tasks.threadSubject: Proposal: Conflict log history table for Logical Replication

tasks.seed: REPACK command with optional CONCURRENTLY flag for concurrent table rewriting
tasks.question: I'm getting up to speed on the REPACK command project. Who is driving this, what patches exist and in what order, what are the main design decisions and open issues, and what compilation/functionality bugs have been reported?
key_points: Alvaro Herrera is leading the effort, building primarily on Antonin Houska's pg_squeeze-based implementation; goal is to unify VACUUM FULL and CLUSTER into a single REPACK command
0001 patch (target: September commitfest for pg19) reshuffles existing CLUSTER code to create basic REPACK command; CONCURRENTLY flag addition deferred to later in pg19 cycle
Robert Treat found bug in cluster.c switch statement: CLUSTER case incorrectly returns 'VACUUM' instead of 'CLUSTER' in the string conversion function
Fujii Masao discovered compilation failure in v17: CLUSTER_COMMAND_CLUSTER identifier used but not declared due to merge mistake when squashing Antonin's 0004 (code restructuring) into 0001
Concurrent mode requires wal_level=logical; later patches (not yet in main series) will support transient enabling of wal_level=logical per-table for concurrent repack

Fujii Masao reported functional failures: 'StartTransactionCommand: unexpected state STARTED' error during repeated REPACK, syntax error on 'REPACK (VERBOSE);', and 'has no identity index' errors when attempting REPACK CONCURRENTLY without proper indexes

Concurrent mode currently marked as non-MVCC safe; Mikhail Nikalayeu considers this a dealbreaker for production use, raising concerns about old snapshots and transactions during the exclusive lock swap phase

Antonin Houska already implemented an MVCC-safe two-phase approach in separate patches (message-id 77690.1725610115@antos) involving logical decoding with shadow table mechanism; Alvaro proposes deferring this for later review with optional flag
VACUUM FULL options ANALYZE and SKIP_DATABASE_STATS are not supported by REPACK (also not by CLUSTER); Antonin suggests documenting this limitation
Mihail Nikalayeu identified semantic overlap between build_new_indexes and index_concurrently_create_copy functions, proposing refactoring; Antonin addressed this in 0002 patch

Patch versioning: Alvaro submitted v17 (not v1, as Antonin had reached v15); earlier problematic version had undeclared identifier; rebased version includes Antonin's 0004 (code restructuring) and 0005 (regression tests)
Identity index requirement is mandatory for REPACK CONCURRENTLY but optional for basic REPACK; USING INDEX syntax supported with or without explicit index name

patches: 0001: REPACK command (reshuffled CLUSTER code)
0002: build_new_indexes refactoring (Antonin)
0004: Code restructuring (Antonin, merged into v17)
0005: Regression tests (merged into v17)
MVCC-safe mode patches (Antonin, message-id 77690.1725610115@antos, deferred)

tasks.seed: pg_rewind timeline divergence handling during multi-crash promotion scenarios
tasks.question: I'm ramping up on pg_rewind's handling of diverging timelines after multiple standby promotions. Who is involved, what is the core issue and proposed fix, what concerns were raised in review, and what remains to be resolved?
key_points: Mats Kindahl identified a scenario where multiple crashes during standby promotion create diverging timelines (e.g., S1 and S2 both create TLI 2 independently), which pg_rewind cannot currently resolve

The specific failure case: S1 crashes, starts promotion to TLI 2, crashes before replicating End-of-Recovery (EOR), then S2 is promoted and also creates TLI 2, creating two independent TLI 2 versions (2.1 and 2.2)
Proposed solution: add UUID tags to timeline history entries to uniquely identify each promotion and allow pg_rewind to distinguish divergent timelines

Surya Poondla identified a critical bug: GetCurrentTimestamp() returns microseconds since Postgres epoch (2000-01-01), but generate_uuidv7_r expects milliseconds since Unix epoch, causing 30-year offset and non-conformant UUIDs
Mats fixed the timestamp issue by switching to gettimeofday() and removed unnecessary EOR record changes that were originally attempted
Logic for zero-UUID handling: if two timelines have identical TLI but one has zero UUID and one doesn't, they are from different promotions (old vs new server) and must be treated as different
Japin Li identified documentation mismatch: comment referenced non-existent timelines_match() function instead of matchAndFetchTimelines()
Struct naming inconsistency: TimelineHistoriesData should be TimeLineHistoriesData for consistency with TimeLineHistoryEntry
Japin Li discovered Windows platform test failure requiring path cleaning in tests (analogous to Cluster.pm pattern)
Patches created at least 5 versions (v1-v5) with iterative improvements based on review feedback
Code refactoring: matchingTimelineUUID() function was created and reused to simplify UUID comparison logic throughout
Test case added: three-promotion divergence scenario to exercise the fix more comprehensively

patches: v1 - Initial patch with UUID tagging of timeline history
v2 - Updated version removing XLOG_END_OF_RECOVERY UUID extension, keeping history file changes
v3 - Timestamp epoch fix using gettimeofday(), EOR changes removed, UUID mismatch error handling added
v4 - Refactored to use matchingTimelineUUID(), fixed typos and naming inconsistencies
v5 - Windows path cleaning fix for tests

tasks.threadSubject: pg_rewind does not rewind diverging timelines

tasks.seed: Logical replication walsender shutdown without remote flush confirmation
tasks.question: Should logical replication walsenders exit before confirming remote flush (unlike physical replication), and what are the design concerns and behavioral implications?
key_points: Current behavior: walsender delays shutdown until remote data flush confirmed (added in commit 985bd7 for physical replication switchover support)
Physical replication use case: stop primary → promote secondary → reboot primary as secondary requires flush confirmation to avoid failed reboot
Logical replication cannot support role switching: if transactions committed during switchover, subscriber cannot request WALs from specific positions and must truncate+recreate subscription
Proposal: logical replication walsender should exit immediately without flush confirmation; physical replication keeps current behavior
Kyotaro Horiguchi concern: breaking existing behavior people may rely on; suggested limiting change to apply_delay case only instead
Kyotaro's alternative: have apply worker send crafted feedback with commit_data.end_lsn as flushpos to trick walsender into thinking flush confirmed
Hayato tested Kyotaro's workaround: failed because sentPtr (next WAL location to send) larger than subscriber's flushed position; end_lsn found in COMMIT record not BEGIN
Hayato's technical concern: delaying in apply_handle_commit() causes apply worker to hold database locks for extended time
Dilip Kumar found typo in patch comment: 'replication more' should be 'replication mode'
Amit Kapila questioned: should WalSndDone() check pq_is_send_pending() for logical replication like caller already does?
Amit Kapila noted: time-delayed logical replication could fill send buffer while apply worker delayed, blocking walsender (unlike physical replication with separate walreceiver process)

patches: walsender logical replication exit patch (multiple revisions with comment fixes)

tasks.threadSubject: Exit walsender before confirming remote flush in logical replication

tasks.question: I'm getting up to speed on improving pg_stat_statements scalability. Who is working on this, what specific scaling problems are being addressed, what patches exist, what design decisions have been made (especially around eviction and parallel safety), and what bugs or concerns remain unresolved?
key_points: Sami Imseih identified three major scaling problems: (1) per-entry SpinLock contention on counter updates, (2) deallocation under exclusive LWLock blocking all backends when hash table is full, (3) query text file bloat triggering GC under exclusive lock
pg_stat_statements.max is a static GUC requiring server restart; deallocation happens inline during query execution and is frequent with many unique queries

Patch series reorganized into v1-0001 (in-transaction flush dependency), v1-0002 (move hash to custom pgstat kind), v1-0003 (DSA + file storage for query text), v1-0004 (new columns to pg_stat_statements_info)

FreeBSD CI failure occurred when parallel workers called pg_stat_statements() marked PARALLEL SAFE; fixed by marking pg_stat_statements() and pg_stat_statements_reset() as PARALLEL RESTRICTED and setting max_parallel_workers_per_gather=0 in pg_stat_statements.conf

Lukas Fittl raised concern about steady workloads losing the 5001st entry when max=5000 if single-threaded eviction blocks new entries; Sami addressed this with parallel clock-sweep eviction using decrementing refcounts

Parallel clock-sweep eviction: multiple backends evict subsets concurrently, decrement refcounts, only entries that decay to zero are evicted; 5% headroom using USAGE_DEALLOC_PERCENT keeps entry count at 95% of max

Zsolt Parragi identified three bugs: (1) minmax_only reset clears mean_time/sum_var_time but recalculates using pre-reset data, (2) min_time doesn't recover after minmax_reset because only updated if smaller but equals zero after reset, (3) possible integer overflow in nentries check lacking explicit uint64 casts similar to deleted code

Lukas Fittl questioned why deferred work on generate_normalized_query if entry confirmed in pgss_store_query_text; also noted tuples_hot_updated and tuples_newpage_updated should respect transaction rollbacks like tuples_(inserted|updated|deleted)
Julien Rouhaud clarified request: maintaining query text save timestamps rather than execution timestamps to identify missing query texts in snapshot-based external systems without full GC
Wiki page created at https://wiki.postgresql.org/wiki/Scalability_of_pg_stat_statements combining 2025 discussion and PGConf.Dev unconference notes
Version bumped to 1.14 for parallel safety changes; concern about backward compatibility if users downgrade pg_stat_statements versions
Partition-based eviction with dshash (dynamic shared hash) and DSA (dynamic shared allocation) used to replace fixed-size hash table and enable concurrent eviction

patches: v1-0001 (in-transaction flush dependency)
v1-0002 (move hash to custom pgstat kind)
v1-0003 (DSA + file storage)
v1-0004 (pg_stat_statements_info columns)
v2 (updated series)
v3 (latest iteration with parallel clock-sweep eviction)
concepts: query text file bloat and garbage collection

tasks.seed: Extended statistics improvement: multi-column MCV missing values
tasks.question: I'm getting up to speed on using MCV statistics for missing value combinations. Who is involved, what patches have been proposed, how do they handle column coverage and estimation, and what are the main concerns about scope and code duplication?
key_points: Enrique Sánchez proposes using MCV miss information: when a multi-column combination is absent from the MCV list, its frequency is bounded by the least common (maximum) value in the MCV list

Zsolt Parragi identified a critical bug: v2 patch failed when clause conditions don't cover all MCV columns (e.g., column 'a' used twice while column 'c' not used), causing significant underestimation
Fix implemented using Bitmapset: compare columns in the clause against stat->keys to ensure all MCV dimensions are covered before applying the cap
v3-0002 adds ndistinct-based estimation: calculates non_mcv_sel = (1.0 - mcv_totalsel) / (ndistinct - mcv_nitems) as upper bound, similar to var_eq_const() in selfuncs.c

Ilia Evdokimov identified code duplication: mcv_can_cap() reimplements logic from dependency_is_compatible_clause(), and get_ndistinct_for_keys() duplicates ndistinct lookup from estimate_multivariate_ndistinct()
Performance issue found: mcv_can_cap() runs unconditionally before is_or branch but is only used in AND branch, causing unnecessary syscache lookups for OR queries
v4-0001 fixes performance by moving mcv_can_cap() and covered_attnums computation inside the else/AND branch only
v4-0002 eliminates duplication by creating helper function for ndistinct lookup used by both MCV and ndistinct statistics code
Design decision: scope limited to 'full-dimensional top-level AND equality miss' for first version; IN/ANY clauses deferred
Design decision: first patch will NOT modify MCV struct to include ndistinct; instead rely on matching extended ndistinct statistics when they exist
Chengpeng Yan and Enrique agreed: top-level AND with equality for all MCV columns has clearest case for using MCV miss information as useful negative information
Enrique's test case demonstrates the planner trap: table where combination (1,1,1) is excluded from MCV but individual values 1 are most common, showing current underestimation

patches: v2: fixed column coverage checking using Bitmapset comparison
v3-0001: added IsA(clause, RestrictInfo) check, added tests
v3-0002: implemented ndistinct-based estimation
v4-0001: moved mcv_can_cap() inside AND branch
v4-0002: created helper function for ndistinct lookup

tasks.threadSubject: Extended statistics improvement: multi-column MCV missing values

tasks.seed: Wait events for server logging destination writes
tasks.question: I'm getting up to speed on instrumenting wait events for server logging operations. Which logging output paths need wait event instrumentation, what WaitEventIO events were added, what build issues came up, and how did the scope evolve across patch versions?
key_points: write(2) calls in write_pipe_chunks() to syslogger pipe and write_console() to stderr were not instrumented; blocking on these showed wait_event = NULL, making logging stalls invisible in pg_stat_activity
v1 had a build failure: elog.c used pgstat_report_wait_start()/end() and WAIT_EVENT_* constants but lacked #include "utils/wait_event.h", exposed only by transitive includes on developer's machine
v2 fixed build failure by adding #include "utils/wait_event.h" to elog.c

Four WaitEventIO events were added: SysloggerWrite (write(2) to syslogger pipe), StderrWrite (write(2) to stderr, WriteConsoleW() on Windows), SyslogWrite (syslog(3) in write_syslog()), EventlogWrite (ReportEventW()/ReportEventA() in write_eventlog())
Michael Paquier noted that normally event numbers are split per code path, but using same numbers for a routine is acceptable here
Kyotaro Horiguchi questioned whether ReportEventW()/ReportEventA() and syslog() should be instrumented if covering all blocking paths; this prompted v3 to extend scope
v3 extended coverage to include syslog(3) calls and Windows ReportEvent calls for consistency across all logging output paths
v4 rebased over current master after conflicts with recent elog.c changes (const WCHAR pointer in write_eventlog()) and new COPY pipe wait events in wait_event_names.txt
Implementation wraps only the leaf write calls using existing pgstat_report_wait_start()/end() helpers, maintaining allocation-free safety for error-reporting paths
Before/after testing showed logging backends previously reported NULL wait events now report IO/SysloggerWrite 99%+ of the time during logging
Series split into v4-0001 (portable: SysloggerWrite, StderrWrite, SyslogWrite) and v4-0002 (Windows: WriteConsoleW, EventlogWrite)
Changes touch only elog.c and wait_event_names.txt, builds clean with Autoconf and Meson on current master

patches: v1
v2 (build fix: added #include)
v3 (extended scope to syslog and ReportEvent)
v4 (rebase over current master)
v4-0001 (portable part)
v4-0002 (Windows part)

tasks.threadSubject: Add wait events for server logging destination writes

tasks.seed: pg_stat_kind_info system view for cumulative statistics introspection
tasks.question: I'm getting up to speed on adding pg_stat_kind_info. Who is involved, what does the patch do, what schema changes were proposed, and what unresolved design questions remain about column naming and NULL handling?
key_points: Tristan Partin proposes pg_stat_kind_info built on pg_stat_get_kind_info() to expose metadata about statistics kinds (id, name, count, builtin, shared_size columns)
Feature addresses lack of SQL introspection for custom statistics registered via pluggable cumulative statistics (commit 7949d959458)
Michael Paquier requires filtering test queries to builtin=true for stable installcheck results when custom kinds are loaded

v1 was missing the patch attachment; v2 was promised with additional columns (fixed_amount, written_to_file, accessed_across_databases, snapshot_ctl_off, shared_ctl_off, shared_data_off, shared_data_len, pending_size) but not yet attached
NULL handling for shared_size debated: should return NULL for built-in fixed-sized stats kinds (unknown value) vs numeric for custom kinds
Tristan proposed renaming write_to_file column to written_to_file or persisted, with question about whether PgStat_KindInfo::write_to_file field name should also change
Michael suggested exposing flags to enable cross-checks: pgstat_register_kind() already validates that fixed_amount+shared_size>0 and !fixed_amount with track_entry_count are forbidden
Track_entry_count returns NULL for count if flag not set, sensible per Michael's review
Discussion of whether callbacks should only be exposed/validated when fixed_amount is true
Michael concerned that built-in stats kinds lack internal consistency mechanism for flags/callbacks setup, custom kinds loaded from libraries need more cross-checks

patches: pg_stat_kind_info patch v1 (referenced but not attached)
pg_stat_kind_info patch v2 (referenced but not attached)

tasks.question: I'm about to work on elog.c. What recent pgsql-hackers activity touches it — which patches/threads/people, and what concerns or changes should I know?
key_points: elog.c was missing #include "utils/wait_event.h" in initial patch versions, causing CI build failures on Linux and Windows until v2 added it
Four new WaitEventIO events are being added: SysloggerWrite, StderrWrite, SyslogWrite (syslog(3) in write_syslog()), and EventlogWrite (Windows ReportEvent variants in write_eventlog())
All wait instrumentation uses pgstat_report_wait_start()/end() helpers placed only around leaf calls to remain allocation-free and safe on error-reporting paths
The patch series is split into two parts: 0001 covers portable logging paths (SysloggerWrite, StderrWrite, SyslogWrite) and 0002 covers Windows-specific paths (WriteConsoleW and EventlogWrite)
Recent rebase conflicts occurred in write_eventlog() due to upstream changes converting to const WCHAR pointer and pgindent formatting, plus new COPY pipe wait events in wait_event_names.txt
EventlogWrite is Windows-only and had limited runtime testing availability—only compile verification via cfbot's Windows task
The series maintains consistent coverage: each event wraps an entire routine (e.g., SyslogWrite covers all syslog(3) calls in write_syslog(), not individual call sites)
Changes touch only two files: elog.c and wait_event_names.txt

patches: v1, v2, v3, v4 - iterative revisions of 'Add wait events for server logging destination writes'
v4-0001 - portable part (SysloggerWrite, StderrWrite, SyslogWrite)
v4-0002 - Windows part (WriteConsoleW plus EventlogWrite)

tasks.question: I'm about to work on cluster.c. What recent pgsql-hackers activity touches it — which patches/threads/people, and what concerns or changes should I know?
key_points: REPACK command is being added to cluster.c alongside existing CLUSTER and VACUUM FULL functionality, intended to help users transition away from deprecated commands
Early WIP patch has a known bug: switch statement case for CLUSTER_COMMAND_CLUSTER incorrectly returns 'VACUUM' instead of 'CLUSTER'
Compilation failure exists: undeclared identifier 'CLUSTER_COMMAND_CLUSTER' in cluster_is_permitted_for_relation() call; may need to use GetUserId() instead
REPACK CONCURRENTLY variant being designed with requirement for wal_level=logical, while plain REPACK must work with wal_level=minimal/replica for backward compatibility
Discussion of eventual deprecation path: VACUUM FULL and CLUSTER may be deprecated in favor of REPACK, requiring compatibility maintenance
cluster_is_permitted_for_relation() function is being integrated into REPACK permission checking logic
Patch is acknowledged as early-stage WIP not ready for full review, with overlapping changes expected once concurrent variant is added

patches: REPACK [concurrently] feature patch (early WIP)

tasks.question: I'm about to work on makefuncs.c. What recent pgsql-hackers activity touches it — which patches/threads/people, and what concerns or changes should I know?
key_points: A refactoring proposal was made (2026-06-01) to unify internal expression construction logic by consolidating wrapper functions.
The proposal targeted make_andclause(), make_orclause(), and make_notclause() to call makeBoolExpr() directly instead of duplicate implementations.
makeSimpleA_Expr() was proposed for removal as a trivial wrapper around makeA_Expr(), and makeDefElemExtended() as a wrapper around makeDefElem().
Tom Lane reviewed and rejected the proposal on 2026-06-01 11:56 UTC, stating 'I don't think this is worth the trouble.'
The patch author (ZizhuanLiu Liu) accepted the rejection and agreed to drop the refactoring idea.
The motivation cited was alignment with existing patterns in gram.y (makeAndExpr/makeOrExpr/makeNotExpr) and elimination of redundant code.
The refactoring was characterized as zero functional change—pure code cleanup with minimal intrusion and no altered runtime behavior.
The proposal was motivated by review of commit cf6723 (Make transformAExprIn() return a flattened bool expression directly).
Test cases were mentioned as being added for refactored functions to prevent regression.

patches: cf6723: Make transformAExprIn() return a flattened bool expression directly (referenced context)
